title: Existence and uniqueness of fractal-fractional equations generated by a new fractal-fractional operator utilizing the advanced gamma function
author: Alazman, Ibrahim

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: The particular circumstances of the equations and the corresponding theorems determine the existence and uniqueness of solutions to differential equations. The standards for determining these attributes may vary throughout differential equation categories. In order to analyze and solve differential equations, the use of theorems such as the Picard-Lindelof theorem and uniqueness theorem is essential. In this section, we consider the following abstract fractal-fractional differential equation(1)bCΔtκ,νυ(t)=V(t,υ(t));or(2)bRLΔtκ,νυ(t)=V(t,υ(t)),where υ(0)=υ0 and V:[0,℘]×R→R is continuous and differentiable. Consider the Banach space B of all continuous functions over the interval J=[0,℘],℘<∞ subjecting to the sup norm.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Next result shows the existence of nonzero solution for Eq. (1).

steps.title: admits a solution υ
steps.instruction: admits a solution υ. In addition, if V is a Lipschitz with respect to the second variable∥V(t,υ(t))−V(t,ψ(t))∥≤l∥υ(t)−ψ(t)∥,such thatl〈1ν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1b2℘b+κb2b+κ,ν〉0then the solution is unique.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Define the following operator: Q:S→S, whereS≔{υ∈B:∥υ∥≤2|υ0|1−Lν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1(b2℘b+κb2b+κ)},t∈[0,℘],℘<∞ andLν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1(b2℘b+κb2b+κ)<1 byQ(υ)(t)=υ0+ν∫0tV(τ,υ(τ))[Πκ]b(−λ(t−τ)κbdτ.Then we have|Q(υ)(t)|=|υ0+ν∫0tV(τ,υ(τ))[Πκ]b(−λ(t−τ)κbdτ|≤|υ0|+ν|∫0tV(τ,υ(τ))[Πκ]b(−λ(t−τ)κbdτ|≤|υ0|+ν∫0t|V(τ,υ(τ))||[Πκ]b(−λ(t−τ)κb|dτ≤|υ0|+νL∥υ∥∫0t|((t−τ)b)(κ/b+1)/b−1Ξ(1+κ/bb,1+κ/bb)(−λ(t−τ)(1+κ/b)/bb(1+κ/b)/b)|dτ≤|υ0|+νL∥υ∥(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1×∫0t|(t−τ))(κ/b+1)/b−1Ξ(1+κ/bb,1+κ/bb)(−λ(t−τ)(1+κ/b)/bb(1+κ/b)/b)|dτ≤|υ0|+νL∥υ∥(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1∫0t|(t−τ))(κ/b+1)/b−1|dτ≤|υ0|+νL∥υ∥(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1(b2℘b+κb2b+κ)Thus, we obtain|Q(υ)(t)|≤|υ0|1−Lν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1(b2℘b+κb2b+κ),υ0≠0.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: By taking the supremum of the above inequality, we have Q∈S (boundedness of Q). We proceed to show that Q is continuous in S. Since V is continuous in J then it is uniformly continuous satisfying∥V(t,υ(t))−V(t,ψ(t))∥≤ϵν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1b2℘b+κb2b+κ.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: A computation brings|Q(υ)(t)−Q(ψ)(t)|≤ν∫0t|V(τ,υ(τ))−V(τ,ψ(τ))||[Πκ]b(−λ(t−τ)κb|dτ≤ν∥V(t,υ(t))−V(t,ψ(t))∥∫0t|((t−τ)b)(κ/b+1)/b−1Ξ(1+κ/bb,1+κ/bb)(−λ(t−τ)(1+κ/b)/bb(1+κ/b)/b)|dτ≤ν∥V(t,υ(t))−V(t,ψ(t))∥(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1×∫0t|(t−τ))(κ/b+1)/b−1Ξ(1+κ/bb,1+κ/bb)(−λ(t−τ)(1+κ/b)/bb(1+κ/b)/b)|dτ≤∥V(t,υ(t))−V(t,ψ(t))∥ν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1∫0t|(t−τ))(κ/b+1)/b−1|dτ≤ενν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1b2℘b+κb2b+κ(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1b2℘b+κb2b+κ=ε.Let t1,t2∈J such that t1≠t2. Then|Q(υ)(t1)−Q(υ)(t2)|≤2|υ0|+ν∫0t1|V(τ,υ(τ))||[Πκ]b(−λ(t1−τ)κb|dτ+ν∫0t2|V(τ,υ(τ))||[Πκ]b(−λ(t2−τ)κb|dτ≤2|υ0|+Lν∥υ∥∫0t1|((t1−τ)b)(κ/b+1)/b−1Ξ(1+κ/bb,1+κ/bb)(−λ(t1−τ)(1+κ/b)/bb(1+κ/b)/b)|dτ+Lν∥υ∥∫0t2|((t2−τ)b)(κ/b+1)/b−1Ξ(1+κ/bb,1+κ/bb)(−λ(t2−τ)(1+κ/b)/bb(1+κ/b)/b)|dτ≤2|υ0|+νL∥υ∥(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1(∫0t1|(t1−τ))(κ/b+1)/b−1|dτ+∫0t2|(t2−τ))(κ/b+1)/b−1|dτ)≤2|υ0|+2νL∥υ∥(1b)κb+1b−1(b2℘b+κb2b+κ)Thus, we obtain|Q(υ)(t1)−Q(υ)(t2)|≤2|υ0|1−Lν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1(b2℘b+κb2b+κ),υ0≠0.Thus, Q is eqicontinuous in S. As a consequence, Arzela-Ascoli theorem yields that Q(S) is relatively compact operator. The Schauder fixed point theorem possesses a fixed point which is corresponding to the solution of (1).

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: For the uniqueness, we have to employ the Banach fixed point theorem.|Q(υ)(t)−Q(ψ)(t)|≤ν∫0t|V(τ,υ(τ))−V(τ,ψ(τ))||[Πκ]b(−λ(t−τ)κb|dτ≤∥V(t,υ(t))−V(t,ψ(t))∥ν∫0t|((t−τ)b)(κ/b+1)/b−1Ξ(1+κ/bb,1+κ/bb)(−λ(t−τ)(1+κ/b)/bb(1+κ/b)/b)|dτ≤∥V(t,υ(t))−V(t,ψ(t))∥ν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1×∫0t|(t−τ))(κ/b+1)/b−1Ξ(1+κ/bb,1+κ/bb)(−λ(t−τ)(1+κ/b)/bb(1+κ/b)/b)|dτ≤∥V(t,υ(t))−V(t,ψ(t))∥ν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1∫0t|(t−τ))(κ/b+1)/b−1|dτ≤(ℓν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1b2℘b+κb2b+κ)∥υ−ψ∥,where(lν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1b2℘b+κb2b+κ)<1.Hence, the operator Q is a contraction mapping, then the Banach fixed point theorem shows that Q has a single fixed point that relates to the outcome of Eq. (1).

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Example 2. Consider the following equation(3)1CΔt1/2,1/2υ(t)=18υ(t);t∈[0,1], subjected initially to υ0=1/2 with l=L=1/8 and ν=κ=1/2 and ℘=b=1. Since,Lν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1(b2℘b+κb2b+κ)=18×12×23=124 l=18.Hence, it admits a unique solution with the formulaυ(t)=υ0+ν∫0tV(τ,υ(τ))[Πκ]b(−λ(t−τ)κbdτ=12+12∫0tυ(τ)8[Π12]1(−λ(t−τ)12dτ.Now, let b=2, thenLν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1(b2℘b+κb2b+κ)=0.125(1b)(b+0.5)b2−3)(b+0.5)=0.12968 l=0.125.Hence, it admits a unique solution. The solution takes the formulaυ(t)=υ0+ν∫0tV(τ,υ(τ))[Πκ]b(−λ(t−τ)κbdτ=12+12∫0tυ(τ)8[Π12]2(−λ(t−τ)14dτ.When b=3, we getLν(1b)(κ/b+1)/b−1(b2℘b+κb2b+κ)=0.314505 l=0.125.Hence, it admits a unique solution. The solution takes the formulaυ(t)=υ0+∫0tV(τ,υ(τ))[Πκ]b(−λ(t−τ)κbdτ=12+12∫0tυ(τ)8[Π12]3(−λ(t−τ)16dτ.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Remark 1 Example 2 is a special type of Fractal-fractional Riccati equation. In several distinct domains, including differential geometry, mathematical physics, and control theory, the Riccati equation exists. It could sometimes be feasible to find closed-form solutions to the general Riccati problem, given its seemingly straightforward nature. The Riccati equation is commonly used in control theory to analyze optimum control issues, where finding the solution is crucial to identifying the best control tactics. It occurs in nonlinear differential equation problems in mathematical physics, and its solutions are frequently important for comprehending the movements of physical systems.
